title: Thus he abides contemplating the body as a body internally; or he abides contemplating the body as a body externally; or he abides contemplating the…
content: Thus he abides contemplating the body as a body internally; or he abides contemplating the body as a body externally; or he abides contemplating the body as a body both internally and externally. Or he abides contemplating the nature of arising in the body; or he abides contemplating the nature of passing away in the body; or he abides contemplating the nature of both arising and passing away in the body. ‘There is a body,’ he establishes mindfulness just enough for knowledge and mindfulness. And he abides independent, not clinging to anything in the world. Thus, monks, a monk abides contemplating the body as a body.”

title: “‘My mind will be internally established, well fixed. Evil, unwholesome states that arise will not invade my mind and will not stand.’ Thus, monk, you…
content: “‘My mind will be internally established, well fixed. Evil, unwholesome states that arise will not invade my mind and will not stand.’ Thus, monk, you should train.”

title: “Monks, not knowing and not seeing the eye as it actually is; not knowing and not seeing forms … eye-consciousness … eye-contact as it actually…
content: “Monks, not knowing and not seeing the eye as it actually is; not knowing and not seeing forms … eye-consciousness … eye-contact as it actually is; not knowing and not seeing the feeling—whether pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—that arises dependent on eye-contact as it actually is, then he becomes attached to the eye, to forms, to eye-consciousness, to eye-contact. Dependent on eye-contact, the feeling—pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—arises; he becomes attached to that feeling. When he dwells contemplating gratification, attached, tied, infatuated, the five aggregates of clinging accumulate for the future. And his craving—which leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting here and there—increases. His bodily torments increase, his mental torments increase, his bodily fevers increase, his mental fevers increase, his bodily pains increase, his mental pains increase. He experiences bodily suffering and mental suffering.”

title: “One who finds no core among becomings, Like one picking flowers from a fig tree — That monk abandons the near and far shore, Like…
content:
“One who finds no core among becomings,
Like one picking flowers from a fig tree —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”

“One in whom there is no anger inside,
Who has gone beyond existence and non-existence —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”

“One whose thoughts have been dispersed,
Internally well-ordered, without remainder —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”

title: “And how, monks, is dwelling heedfully? If one lives with restraint of the eye faculty, monks, his mind is not defiled by forms cognizable by…
content: “And how, monks, is dwelling heedfully? If one lives with restraint of the eye faculty, monks, his mind is not defiled by forms cognizable by the eye. For one with an undefiled mind, gladness arises. With gladness, rapture arises. With rapture, the body becomes tranquil. With a tranquil body, he experiences ease. For one at ease, the mind becomes concentrated. When the mind is concentrated, phenomena are clearly seen. When phenomena are clearly seen, he is called ‘one who dwells heedfully’ … the ear faculty … the nose faculty … the tongue faculty … the body faculty … If one lives with restraint of the mind faculty, his mind is not defiled by phenomena cognizable by the mind. For one with an undefiled mind, gladness arises … he is called ‘one who dwells heedfully.’ Thus, monks, is dwelling heedfully.”

title: “Migajāla, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in them, praises…
content: “Migajāla, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in them, praises them, and dwells with lust for them, then because he delights, praises, and dwells with lust, delight arises. With the arising of delight, lust arises. With the arising of lust, bondage arises. Bound by the bondage of delight, Migajāla, that monk is called ‘one who dwells with a companion.’ Even if he lives in remote lodgings, in forests, in groves, in quiet places, with little sound, little noise, free from people’s breath, secluded, suitable for seclusion — still he is called ‘one who dwells with a companion.’ Why? Because craving, the companion, has not been abandoned by him. Therefore he is called ‘one who dwells with a companion.’”

title: “Householder, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in…
content: “Householder, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in those forms, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them, not depending on them, consciousness arises without clinging to them. Householder, a monk without clinging attains complete Nibbāna … Householder, there are tastes … Householder, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable … enticing. If a monk does not delight in those phenomena, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — because he does not delight, does not praise, and dwells without lust, not depending on them, consciousness arises without clinging to them. Without clinging, householder, that monk attains Nibbāna. This, householder, is the cause, this is the reason why some beings here and now attain complete Nibbāna.”

title: “And how, monks, is a monk one who sees sensual pleasures? When sensual pleasures are seen, then desire, lust for sensual pleasure, infatuation with sensual…
content: “And how, monks, is a monk one who sees sensual pleasures? When sensual pleasures are seen, then desire, lust for sensual pleasure, infatuation with sensual pleasure, and fever of sensual pleasure have no underlying tendency regarding sensual pleasures. Suppose, monks, there is a pit of glowing embers deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing embers that are neither flaming nor smoking. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering. Two strong men grab him by both arms and drag him toward that pit. He twists his body this way and that. Why? Because he knows: ‘I will fall into this pit of embers. Because of this, I will meet death or deadly suffering.’ So too, monks, a monk sees sensual pleasures as a pit of embers. And one who sees sensual pleasures thus has no desire, lust, infatuation, or fever for sensual pleasures as an underlying tendency.”

title: “And how, good sirs, is one not defiled by sensual lust? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he is…
content: “And how, good sirs, is one not defiled by sensual lust? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he is not attached to agreeable forms nor repelled by disagreeable forms. He dwells with bodily mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He knows as it actually is the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Here, evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are abandoned without remainder. When he tastes a flavor with the tongue … When he cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, he is not attached to agreeable phenomena nor repelled by disagreeable phenomena. He dwells with bodily mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He knows as it actually is the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Here, evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are abandoned without remainder. This, good sirs, is called a monk who is not defiled by sensual lust regarding forms … regarding tastes … regarding phenomena. A monk living thus, good sirs, if Māra approaches him through the eye, Māra does not gain an opportunity, Māra does not gain an object. If Māra approaches him through the tongue … through the mind, Māra does not gain an opportunity, Māra does not gain an object.”

title: “Monks, on King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where neither monkeys nor humans go. On King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches…
content: “Monks, on King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where neither monkeys nor humans go. On King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where monkeys go but humans do not. On King Snowy Mountain there are even, pleasant stretches where both monkeys and humans go. There, monks, hunters set a trap of sticky resin on the paths where monkeys go, to catch monkeys. Here, monks, those monkeys that are not foolish and not greedy avoid the trap of resin when they see it. But a foolish, greedy monkey, seeing the resin, reaches out with its hand and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hand, it reaches out with the other hand and gets stuck. Wanting to free both hands, it reaches out with one foot and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hands and foot, it reaches out with the other foot and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hands and feet, it puts its mouth to it and gets stuck. Thus, monks, the monkey, caught in five places, lies down screeching, having fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the hunter’s will. The hunter then kills it, roasts it over a charcoal fire, and goes away as he wishes. So too, monks, for one who wanders outside his own proper range, into the domain of others. For one who wanders outside his proper range, into the domain of others, Māra gains an opportunity, Māra gains an object.”
